title: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.title: Understanding Bile Composition Changes from Hepatic to Gallbladder Bile
items.content: Bile composition changes significantly as it transitions from hepatic bile, which is isotonic with blood plasma, to gallbladder bile. This transformation occurs due to the reabsorption of inorganic anions such as chloride and bicarbonate across the gallbladder epithelium, leading to a higher total solute concentration in gallbladder bile.
keyPoints: Hepatic bile has a solute concentration of 3–4 g/dL.
Gallbladder bile's solute concentration increases to 10–15 g/dL due to water reabsorption.
Major components: Bile acids (80%), phospholipids (16%), unesterified cholesterol (4.0%).
In lithogenic state, cholesterol can be as high as 8–10%.

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.title: Mechanisms Regulating Bile Flow in Hepatocytes
items.content: Bile flow is regulated by three key mechanisms: active transport of bile acids into bile canaliculi, active transport of other organic anions, and cholangiocellular secretion. The latter mechanism involves a secretin-mediated process that increases bicarbonate-rich fluid secretion into the bile ducts.
keyPoints: Active transport of bile acids is crucial for bile flow regulation.
Cholangiocellular secretion is secretin-mediated and cyclic AMP-dependent.
Bicarbonate-rich fluid is secreted into the bile ducts.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.title: Genetic Defects Affecting Biliary Excretion and Cholestasis
items.content: Several genetic defects in transport proteins can lead to cholestasis or impaired biliary excretion. These include mutations in F1C1, BSEP, MRP2, MDR3, ABCG5/G8, and CFTR, each associated with specific liver diseases.
keyPoints: F1C1 (ATP8B1) defect leads to PFIC1 and BRIC1.
BSEP (ABCB11) defect causes PFIC2 and BRIC2.
MRP2 (ABCC2) mutations result in Dubin-Johnson syndrome.
MDR3 (ABCB4) defect results in PFIC3.
ABCG5/G8 defects cause sitosterolemia.
CFTR (ABCC7) defect is associated with cystic fibrosis and chronic cholestatic liver disease.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.title: Bile Acid Composition and Solubility in Bile
items.content: Primary bile acids, cholic acid and CDCA, are synthesized from cholesterol and conjugated with glycine or taurine. The ratio of these conjugates is approximately 3:1 in healthy individuals. Bile acids form micelles above a critical concentration (~2 mM), which solubilize cholesterol and facilitate fat absorption.
keyPoints: Primary bile acids are synthesized from cholesterol and conjugated with glycine or taurine.
The ratio of glycine to taurine conjugates in bile is ~3:1.
Bile acids form micelles above a critical concentration of ~2 mM, aiding in cholesterol solubility.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.title: Role of Bile Acids in Intestinal Absorption and Hormonal Function
items.content: Bile acids facilitate the absorption of dietary fats and fat-soluble vitamins via micellar transport. They also act as hormones by binding to FXR and TGR5 receptors, regulating bile acid metabolism and enterohepatic circulation.
keyPoints: Bile acids aid in the intestinal absorption of dietary fats and fat-soluble vitamins.
They function as hormones via FXR and TGR5 receptor binding.
Regulation involves nuclear receptors like FXR, which upregulates BSEP and represses bile acid synthesis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.title: Enterohepatic Circulation of Bile Acids
items.content: Bile acids are conserved through active transport in the distal ileum, with a normal pool size of ~2–4 g. They circulate 5-10 times daily and have a fecal loss of 0.2–0.4 g per day, balanced by hepatic synthesis. FGF19 regulates bile acid synthesis by inhibiting CYP7A1.
keyPoints: Normal bile acid pool size is ~2–4 g, circulating 5-10 times daily.
Daily fecal loss of bile acids is 0.2–0.4 g, matched by hepatic synthesis.
FGF19 inhibits CYP7A1 to regulate bile acid synthesis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.title: Role of Cholecystokinin in Gallbladder Function
items.content: Cholecystokinin (CCK) is crucial for gallbladder evacuation, as it induces contraction of the gallbladder and reduces sphincter of Oddi resistance, facilitating bile flow into the duodenum.
keyPoints: CCK is released in response to fats and amino acids ingestion.
CCK actions: (1) contracts the gallbladder, (2) decreases SOD resistance, (3) enhances biliary content flow.
Normal gallbladder capacity is ~30 mL.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: Clinical Implications of Congenital Gallbladder Anomalies
items.content: Congenital anomalies such as a 'floating' gallbladder can predispose to acute torsion, volvulus, or herniation due to abnormal positioning.
keyPoints: Common biliary tract anomalies include agenesis, duplications, and positional abnormalities.
'Floating' gallbladder increases risk of torsion, volvulus, or herniation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.title: Epidemiology of Gallstones
items.content: Gallstones are prevalent, especially after age 50, with higher incidence in Native Americans and women. Understanding the epidemiological distribution aids in identifying at-risk populations.
keyPoints: Prevalence increases after age 50.
Highest among Native Americans, followed by Hispanics, non-Hispanic whites, and black Americans.
Higher prevalence in women than men across all ages.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Cholesterol Gallstones
items.content: Cholesterol gallstones form due to an imbalance between cholesterol, phospholipids, and bile acids. Key mechanisms include increased biliary secretion of cholesterol and genetic factors.
keyPoints: Cholesterol stones account for >90% in Western countries.
Formed from unstable, cholesterol-rich vesicles when there's excess cholesterol relative to phospholipids and bile acids.
Increased biliary cholesterol secretion linked with obesity, high-caloric diets, and certain drugs.
Genetic factors contribute 25% to gallstone pathogenesis; SNP in ABCG5/G8 gene found in 21% of patients.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.title: Role of Genetic and Environmental Factors
items.content: Both genetic predispositions and environmental factors like diet contribute to gallstone formation. Genetic studies highlight specific gene mutations associated with increased risk.
keyPoints: Genetic factors account for 25% of variation in monozygotic twins.
Shared environmental factors: 13%, individual environmental factors: 62%.
SNP in ABCG5/G8 gene found more frequently in gallstone patients (21%) than general population (9%).
Mutations in CYP7A1 and MDR3 genes linked to cholesterol metabolism disturbances.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Cholesterol Gallstone Formation
items.content: Cholesterol gallstones form due to nucleation and growth of cholesterol monohydrate crystals, influenced by the balance between pronucleating factors like mucin and immunoglobulins, and antinucleating factors such as apolipoproteins A-I and A-II. Gallbladder hypomotility further contributes to stone formation.
keyPoints: Nucleation of cholesterol monohydrate crystals is accelerated in lithogenic bile due to an excess of pronucleating factors or a deficiency of antinucleating factors.
Pigment particles and the UGT1A1 gene variant are implicated in gallstone pathogenesis, particularly with cholesterol stones.
Gallbladder hypomotility leads to incomplete emptying of supersaturated bile, promoting stone growth.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.title: Role and Implications of Biliary Sludge
items.content: Biliary sludge is a precursor to gallstones, characterized by the presence of lecithin-cholesterol liquid crystals and cholesterol monohydrate crystals. It indicates bile supersaturation and can lead to gallstone formation or biliary pain.
keyPoints: Biliary sludge consists of lecithin-cholesterol liquid crystals, cholesterol monohydrate crystals, calcium bilirubinate, and mucin gels.

In a study, 96% of patients with gallbladder sludge experienced changes over time: disappearance (60%), recurrence (18%), development into gallstones (14%), or severe biliary pain/acute pancreatitis (6%).
Biliary sludge is associated with conditions causing gallbladder hypomotility and implies bile supersaturation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.title: Prevention of Gallstones in Rapid Weight Loss
items.content: During rapid weight reduction, such as very-low-calorie diets, the risk of gallstone formation can be significantly reduced by administering ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA).
keyPoints: 10–20% of individuals on a very-low-calorie diet develop gallstones.
In a study with 600 patients on a 520-kcal/d diet, UDCA at 600 mg/d reduced gallstone formation to 3%, compared to 28% in placebo-treated patients.
UDCA also reduces the risk of gallstone formation from 30% to 8% within 6 months in obese patients undergoing gastric banding.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.title: Risk Factors and Pathogenesis of Cholesterol Gallstones
items.content: Cholesterol gallstones are influenced by demographic/genetic factors, obesity/metabolic syndrome, rapid weight loss, hormonal influences, age, gallbladder hypomotility, clofibrate therapy, decreased bile acid secretion, and phospholipid secretion. These factors lead to increased biliary cholesterol secretion or decreased bile salt and phospholipid levels, promoting stone formation.
keyPoints: Prevalence highest in North American Indians, Chilean Indians, and Chilean Hispanics; lowest in Japan
Obesity leads to increased biliary secretion of cholesterol
Rapid weight loss increases biliary cholesterol secretion while decreasing bile acid circulation
Estrogens increase hepatic uptake of dietary cholesterol and biliary cholesterol secretion
Pregnancy causes impaired gallbladder emptying due to progesterone and estrogens
Increasing age results in increased biliary cholesterol secretion and decreased bile salt pool
Gallbladder hypomotility from factors like total parenteral nutrition, fasting, pregnancy, and drugs such as octreotide leads to stasis
Clofibrate therapy increases biliary cholesterol secretion
Genetic defects in CYP7A1 gene decrease bile acid secretion
Genetic defect in MDR3 gene decreases phospholipid secretion
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.title: Hormonal and Lifestyle Influences on Cholesterol Gallstone Formation
items.content: Hormones such as estrogens and progesterone, along with lifestyle factors like rapid weight loss and high-calorie diets, significantly impact cholesterol gallstone formation. Estrogens increase biliary cholesterol secretion while decreasing bile salt conversion to cholesteryl esters. Progesterone impairs gallbladder emptying during pregnancy, increasing the risk of stone formation.
keyPoints: Estrogens and oral contraceptives decrease bile salt secretion
Progesterone impairs gallbladder emptying in combination with estrogen effects
Rapid weight loss increases biliary cholesterol secretion due to mobilization of tissue cholesterol
High-calorie, high-fat diets contribute to the formation of cholesterol stones
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.title: Risk Factors for Pigment Stone Formation
items.content: Pigment stones are more common in certain demographic and genetic contexts, such as Asia and rural settings due to parasitic infections. Other risk factors include chronic hemolysis, alcohol-related liver cirrhosis, ineffective erythropoiesis, cystic fibrosis, chronic biliary tract infection, increasing age, and ileal disease or resection.
keyPoints: Pigment stones are frequent in Asia due to parasitic infections.
Associated with conditions like sickle cell disease, pernicious anemia, and cystic fibrosis.
Risk increases with chronic biliary tract infection and increasing age.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Imaging for Cholelithiasis
items.content: Ultrasonography is the preferred method for diagnosing cholelithiasis, capable of identifying stones as small as 1.5 mm with high accuracy (~2-4% false-negative/positive rates). It can also assess gallbladder emptying function and distinguish between biliary sludge and stones.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography is highly accurate for detecting cholelithiasis, identifying stones as small as 1.5 mm.
False-negative and false-positive rates are ~2-4%.
Can assess gallbladder emptying function.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.title: Symptoms and Diagnosis of Gallstone Disease
items.content: Gallstones typically cause symptoms by inducing inflammation or obstruction. Biliary colic, a severe steady ache in the epigastric or right upper quadrant, is characteristic. Symptoms persisting beyond 5 hours may indicate acute cholecystitis. Nausea and vomiting often accompany biliary pain, while fever or chills suggest complications.
keyPoints: Biliary colic is a key symptom of gallstone disease, characterized by severe steady ache.
Symptoms lasting over 5 hours may suggest acute cholecystitis.
Nausea and vomiting are common with biliary pain; fever or chills indicate complications.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.title: Indications for Cholecystectomy in Asymptomatic Gallstone Patients
items.content: Cholecystectomy is generally not recommended for asymptomatic gallstone patients due to the low risk of developing symptoms or complications. However, surgery may be considered if the patient experiences frequent or severe symptoms interfering with daily life, has a history of gallstone-related complications, or possesses an underlying condition that increases the risk of future complications.
keyPoints: 60-80% of asymptomatic patients remain symptom-free over 25 years.
2–4% per year probability of developing symptoms within 5 years; decreases to 1–2% thereafter.
Yearly incidence of complications is about 0.1–0.3%.
Prophylactic cholecystectomy not warranted for asymptomatic patients.
Consider surgery if: frequent/severe symptoms, prior gallstone complications, or predisposing conditions.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.title: Advantages and Safety of Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy
items.content: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is preferred for symptomatic cholelithiasis due to its minimal invasiveness, reduced hospital stay, lower disability, and cost-effectiveness. It has a low complication rate, with a conversion to open surgery in 5% of cases, a death rate <0.1%, and bile duct injury rates comparable to open cholecystectomy (0.2–0.6%).
keyPoints: Complications occur in ~4% of patients undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Conversion to laparotomy occurs in 5% of cases.
Death rate is <0.1%.
Bile duct injury rates are 0.2–0.6%, comparable with open surgery.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.title: Indications and Success Rates for Gallstone Dissolution Therapy
items.content: UDCA therapy is effective in dissolving radiolucent gallstones <10 mm, with a ~50% success rate within 6 months to 2 years. However, it is most effective for stones <5 mm.
keyPoints: Complete dissolution of radiolucent stones <10 mm can be achieved in ~50% of patients over 6 months to 2 years.
Best results are seen with stones <5 mm.
UDCA dose: 10–15 mg/kg per day.
<10% of symptomatic cholelithiasis patients are candidates for this treatment.
Recurrent stones occur in 30–50% of cases over 3–5 years.
Pigment stones do not respond to UDCA therapy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.title: Considerations and Limitations for Gallstone Dissolution Therapy
items.content: While gallstone dissolution can be an option, its role is limited due to the high recurrence rate of stones and the availability of laparoscopic cholecystectomy. UDCA therapy may be considered for patients who cannot undergo surgery or prefer non-surgical options.
keyPoints: Stones >10 mm rarely dissolve with UDCA therapy.
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy has reduced the role of dissolution therapy.
Patients with recurrent choledocholithiasis post-cholecystectomy may benefit from long-term UDCA treatment.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.title: Diagnosis and Imaging in Acute Cholecystitis
items.content: Acute cholecystitis is typically diagnosed based on clinical history, physical examination, and imaging. The classic triad of RUQ tenderness, fever, and leukocytosis suggests the diagnosis. Ultrasound is highly effective, demonstrating calculi in 90-95% of cases and signs of inflammation such as gallbladder wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid.
keyPoints: Leukocytosis typically ranges from 10,000–15,000 cells/μL with a left shift.
Serum bilirubin is mildly elevated (<85.5 μmol/L) in fewer than half of patients.
Ultrasound shows calculi in 90-95% and signs of inflammation like wall thickening.
HIDA scan may confirm diagnosis if bile ducts are visualized without gallbladder visualization.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.title: Management and Complications of Acute Cholecystitis
items.content: Most patients with acute cholecystitis experience symptom remission within 2-7 days with medical treatment. However, 25% may develop complications requiring surgical intervention. Early surgery is recommended to prevent recurrence; approximately 25% will have a recurrence within one year and 60% within six years.
keyPoints: 75% of patients experience symptom remission within 2-7 days with medical treatment.
25% of medically treated patients develop complications needing surgical intervention.
Recurrence rates are ~25% within one year and 60% within six years.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Mirizzi's Syndrome
items.content: Mirizzi's syndrome is a rare complication where a gallstone impacts the cystic duct or neck of the gallbladder, compressing the CBD and causing jaundice. Diagnosis involves imaging techniques like ultrasound, ERCP, PTC, or MRCP to identify extrinsic compression of the CBD.
keyPoints: Mirizzi's syndrome involves a gallstone impacting the cystic duct or neck, compressing the CBD.
Ultrasound shows stones outside the hepatic duct; ERCP, PTC, or MRCP demonstrate CBD compression.
Surgical treatment includes removing the cystic duct, diseased gallbladder, and impacted stone.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acalculous Cholecystitis
items.content: Acalculous cholecystitis is characterized by acute gallbladder inflammation without stones, often complicating severe underlying illness. Diagnostic imaging such as ultrasound or CT showing a large, tense, static gallbladder can be useful. The complication rate for acalculous cholecystitis exceeds that of calculous cholecystitis.
keyPoints: Acalculous cholecystitis is associated with severe underlying illness.
Ultrasound/CT may show a large, tense, static gallbladder without stones.
Complication rate for acalculous cholecystitis exceeds that of calculous cholecystitis.
Early diagnosis and surgical intervention are crucial for successful management.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.title: Identification Criteria for Acalculous Cholecystopathy
items.content: Acalculous cholecystopathy can be identified by recurrent episodes of typical right upper quadrant pain, abnormal CCK cholescintigraphy with a gallbladder ejection fraction <40%, and reproduction of the patient's pain upon CCK infusion. Ultrasound may reveal a large gallbladder.
keyPoints: Recurrent RUQ pain characteristic of biliary tract pain.
Abnormal CCK cholescintigraphy showing gallbladder ejection fraction <40%.
CCK infusion reproducing the patient's pain.
Ultrasound may show a large gallbladder.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.title: Management of Empyema and Hydrops in Cholecystitis
items.content: Empyema results from superinfection of stagnant bile due to cystic duct obstruction, presenting with high fever, severe RUQ pain, leukocytosis, and risk of sepsis or perforation. Hydrops or mucocele arises from prolonged cystic duct obstruction, leading to gallbladder distention by mucus or transudate.
keyPoints: Empyema presents with high fever, severe RUQ pain, leukocytosis, and risk of sepsis/perforation.
Hydrops/mucocele results from prolonged cystic duct obstruction.
Cholecystectomy is indicated for both conditions due to risks of empyema, perforation, or gangrene.
Emergency surgical intervention with antibiotics is required for empyema.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.title: Fistula Formation and Gallstone Ileus: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Inflammation from acute cholecystitis can lead to fistula formation, commonly into the duodenum. Asymptomatic biliary-enteric fistulas may be detected by gas in the biliary tree on plain abdominal films. Symptomatic cases require surgical intervention including cholecystectomy and closure of the fistulous tract. Gallstone ileus results from a large gallstone causing intestinal obstruction, typically at the ileocecal valve, often without prior symptoms.
keyPoints: Fistulas into the duodenum are most common, followed by those involving the hepatic flexure of the colon, stomach, jejunum, abdominal wall, and renal pelvis.
Asymptomatic biliary-enteric fistulas occur in up to 5% of patients undergoing cholecystectomy.
Large stones (>2.5 cm) predispose to fistula formation by erosion through the gallbladder fundus.
Diagnostic confirmation may be found on plain abdominal films showing pneumobilia and a calcified, ectopic gallstone.
Laparotomy with stone extraction is the procedure of choice for relieving obstruction in gallstone ileus.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.title: Limey Bile and Porcelain Gallbladder: Clinical Implications
items.content: Calcium precipitation in the gallbladder can lead to limey bile, which is usually clinically innocuous but may prompt cholecystectomy if associated with a hydropic gallbladder. Porcelain gallbladder involves calcium deposition within the gallbladder wall and was historically linked to high cancer risk. However, current understanding differentiates between complete intramural calcification and selective mucosal calcification, with varying cancer risks.
keyPoints: Limey bile is characterized by diffuse opacification of bile or a layering effect on plain abdominal roentgenography.
Cholecystectomy is often performed for limey bile in hydropic gallbladders.
Porcelain gallbladder involves calcium deposition within the wall of a chronically inflamed gallbladder.
Cancer risk varies: higher with selective intramural calcification than complete mucosal wall calcification.
Cholecystectomy is not absolute for porcelain gallbladder; close surveillance is also acceptable.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.title: Management of Severe Acute Cholecystitis Prior to Surgery
items.content: In severe acute cholecystitis, initial management involves in-hospital stabilization before surgical intervention. This includes eliminating oral intake, possibly using nasogastric suction, correcting extracellular volume depletion and electrolyte abnormalities, providing analgesia, and administering intravenous antibiotics.
keyPoints: Oral intake is eliminated to stabilize the patient.
Nasogastric suction may be indicated for severe cases.
Correction of extracellular volume depletion and electrolyte imbalances is crucial.
Analgesia is provided to manage pain.
Intravenous antibiotics are administered even if bacterial superinfection has not yet occurred.
Common organisms targeted include E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Enterobacter, and Streptococcus.
Effective antibiotic regimens: piperacillin plus tazobactam, imipenem, meropenem, ceftriaxone plus metronidazole, levofloxacin plus metronidazole.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.title: Role of Antibiotic Therapy in Reducing Postoperative Complications
items.content: Antibiotic therapy is crucial for patients with severe acute cholecystitis to reduce the risk of postoperative complications such as wound infection, abscess formation, and sepsis. This preemptive treatment targets common pathogens associated with biliary infections.
keyPoints: Intravenous antibiotics are indicated in severe cases to prevent bacterial superinfection.
Antibiotic therapy reduces postoperative complications like wound infection, abscess formation, and sepsis.
Common organisms include E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Enterobacter, and Streptococcus.
Effective antibiotics: piperacillin plus tazobactam, imipenem, meropenem, ceftriaxone plus metronidazole, levofloxacin plus metronidazole.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.title: Optimal Timing for Cholecystectomy
items.content: The timing of surgical intervention for acute cholecystitis should be based on patient stabilization, with a trend toward earlier surgery to reduce hospital stays. Early elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is recommended within 48–72 hours after diagnosis unless contraindicated by the patient's overall medical condition or diagnostic uncertainty.
keyPoints: Early surgery (within 48-72 hours) does not increase complication rates compared to delayed (>6 weeks) surgery.
Mortality for emergency cholecystectomy ranges from 1-3%, while early elective cholecystectomy has a mortality risk of ~0.5% in patients under age 60.
Early cholecystectomy is preferred unless patient's condition or diagnostic uncertainty suggests otherwise.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Management of Seriously Ill Patients
items.content: For seriously ill or debilitated patients with cholecystitis, percutaneous drainage or endoscopic transpapillary/transmural drainage may be used as initial management. Elective cholecystectomy can be performed later once the patient's condition stabilizes.
keyPoints: Percutaneous drainage options include cholecystostomy tube and transpapillary/transmural stents.
Elective surgery is deferred until the patient's medical condition permits.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of SOD Stenosis
items.content: Sphincter of Oddi (SOD) stenosis can cause symptoms resembling biliary colic due to recurrent, intermittent biliary obstruction. Diagnosis involves criteria such as upper abdominal pain, abnormal liver tests, CBD dilatation on imaging, delayed contrast drainage, and increased basal pressure of the SOD.
keyPoints: Five diagnostic criteria for SOD stenosis: RUQ or epigastric pain, abnormal liver tests, CBD dilatation, delayed (>45 min) contrast drainage, increased basal SOD pressure.
Treatment involves endoscopic or surgical sphincteroplasty to ensure patency of bile and pancreatic ducts.
Indications for sphincterotomy include prolonged symptoms, lack of response to treatment, severe disability, and patient preference.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Sphincter of Oddi Dysfunction
items.content: Sphincter of Oddi dysfunction is diagnosed when biliary pain occurs without bile duct stones or other abnormalities, with either elevated liver enzymes or a dilated common bile duct (CBD), but not both. If both are present, consider obstruction. Diagnosis can be supported by hepatobiliary scintigraphy or sphincter of Oddi manometry. Medical treatment with nitrites or anticholinergics may be attempted for 2-3 months if no other cause is found and criteria suggest SOD dyskinesia. Endoscopic biliary sphincterotomy (EBS) is indicated if medical therapy fails, especially with elevated SOD pressures, providing long-term pain relief in about 45% of patients.
keyPoints: Biliary pain without bile duct stones or other abnormalities
Either elevated liver enzymes or a dilated CBD, but not both
Consider obstruction if both are present
Supportive tests: hepatobiliary scintigraphy or SOD manometry
Medical treatment (nitrites/anticholinergics) for 2-3 months
EBS indicated if medical therapy fails and SOD pressures are elevated
Approximately 45% long-term pain relief after EBS
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Management of Postcholecystectomy Diarrhea
items.content: Postcholecystectomy patients may experience diarrhea due to changes in gut transit and bile acid composition. Cholecystectomy accelerates fecal passage, increasing colonic bile acid output and shifting towards more diarrheagenic secondary bile acids like deoxycholic acid. Severe diarrhea, defined as three or more watery movements per day, occurs in 5-10% of patients after elective cholecystectomy. Bile acid-sequestering agents such as cholestyramine or colestipol are effective treatments.
keyPoints: Postcholecystectomy changes in gut transit
Increased colonic bile acid output and shift to secondary bile acids
Severe diarrhea: three or more watery movements per day
Occurs in 5-10% of patients post-elective cholecystectomy
Effective treatment with bile acid-sequestering agents (cholestyramine/colestipol)
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: When to Consider Cholecystectomy in Hyperplastic Cholecystoses
items.content: Cholecystectomy is indicated for adenomyomatosis or cholesterolosis when biliary symptoms are present. For gallbladder polyps, cholecystectomy is recommended for symptomatic patients and asymptomatic patients over 50 years with polyps >10 mm, those associated with gallstones, or showing growth on serial ultrasonography.
keyPoints: Cholecystectomy indicated in adenomyomatosis or cholesterolosis with biliary symptoms
Prevalence of gallbladder polyps: 3–6% with male predominance
Types of polyps: cholesterol polyps, adenomyomas, inflammatory polyps, adenomas (rare)
No significant changes in asymptomatic patients with polyps <6 mm over 5 years
Cholecystectomy recommended for symptomatic patients and asymptomatic >50 years with polyps >10 mm or associated with gallstones
Polyp growth on serial ultrasonography warrants cholecystectomy
Low-risk radiologic features in 10-14 mm polyps may allow monitoring instead of surgery
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Gallbladder Polyps
items.content: Gallbladder polyps have a prevalence of 3–6% in adults with male predominance. Types include cholesterol polyps, adenomyomas, inflammatory polyps, and rare adenomas. Cholecystectomy is advised for symptomatic patients or asymptomatic patients over 50 years with polyps >10 mm, associated with gallstones, or showing growth on serial ultrasonography. Polyps <6 mm show few changes over 5 years; those 7–9 mm also remain stable.
keyPoints: Prevalence of gallbladder polyps: 3–6% in adults
Male predominance observed
Types: cholesterol polyps, adenomyomas, inflammatory polyps, adenomas (rare)
Cholecystectomy for symptomatic patients or asymptomatic >50 years with polyps >10 mm
Polyps associated with gallstones also warrant cholecystectomy
Growth on serial ultrasonography indicates need for surgery
Stability in polyps <6 mm over 5 years; few changes in 7–9 mm
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.question: Which transport system is primarily responsible for the active vectorial trans-hepatocellular movement of bile acids into the bile canaliculi?
items.explanation: The Na+/taurocholate cotransporter (NTCP) is one of the primary transport systems responsible for bile acid uptake into hepatocytes.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.question: What is the major solute component of bile by moles percent?
items.explanation: Bile acids constitute approximately 80% of the major solute components of bile by moles percent.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.question: Which mechanism is secretin-mediated and cyclic AMP-dependent, resulting in the secretion of a bicarbonate-rich fluid into the bile ducts?
options: Active transport of bile acids from hepatocytes into the bile canaliculi
Active transport of other organic anions
items.explanation: Cholangiocellular secretion is a secretin-mediated and cyclic AMP-dependent mechanism that results in the secretion of bicarbonate-rich fluid into bile ducts.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.question: Which transporter defect is associated with progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis type 1 (PFIC1) and benign recurrent intrahepatic cholestasis type 1 (BRIC1)?
items.explanation: Defects in F1C1 (ATP8B1) are associated with PFIC1 and BRIC1, leading to impaired ATP-dependent transporter functions.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.question: Which of the following bile acids are synthesized in hepatocytes from cholesterol?
items.explanation: Cholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid are synthesized in hepatocytes from cholesterol.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.question: What is the primary mechanism for bile salt recirculation in the distal ileum?
items.explanation: The active transport mechanism is quantitatively much more important for bile salt recirculation in the distal ileum.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.question: Which nuclear receptor regulates the expression of hepatic BSEP (ABCB11) and represses bile acid synthesis?
items.explanation: The farnesoid X receptor (FXR) upregulates hepatic BSEP and represses bile acid synthesis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.question: What is the normal daily fecal loss of bile acids in grams?
items.explanation: The normal daily fecal loss of bile acids is in the range of 0.2–0.4 g.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.question: Which hormone is primarily responsible for the contraction of the gallbladder and decreased resistance at the sphincter of Oddi?
items.explanation: Cholecystokinin (CCK) is released in response to fats and amino acids, causing gallbladder contraction and decreased sphincter of Oddi resistance.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.question: What is the normal capacity of the gallbladder?
items.explanation: The normal capacity of the gallbladder is approximately 30 mL.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.question: Which congenital anomaly involves a partial or complete septum separating the fundus from the body of the gallbladder?
items.explanation: A Phrygian cap is a congenital anomaly where a septum or fold separates the fundus from the body of the gallbladder.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.question: Which ethnic group has the highest prevalence of gallstones in the United States?
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence is highest in Native Americans.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of gallstones in Western industrialized countries are cholesterol stones?
items.explanation: Cholesterol stones account for >90% of all gallstones in Western industrialized countries.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary composition of pigment stones?
items.explanation: Pigment stones are composed primarily of calcium bilirubinate.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is associated with a monogenic cause of cholesterol gallstones?
items.explanation: Mutations in the CYP7A1 gene result in a deficiency of cholesterol 7-hydroxylase.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a pronucleating factor for cholesterol monohydrate crystals?
items.explanation: Mucin is identified as a pronucleating factor for cholesterol monohydrate crystals.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.question: What condition increases the risk of gallstone formation due to impaired gallbladder emptying?
items.explanation: Pregnancy is associated with sluggish gallbladder contraction, leading to impaired gallbladder emptying and increased risk of gallstone formation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as contributing to cholesterol stone formation?
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia is not mentioned in the passage as a factor contributing to cholesterol stone formation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.question: What role does UGT1A1 gene variant play in gallstone disease?
options: It is associated with pigment stones.
It leads to cholesterol supersaturation of bile.
It is linked to the presence of cholesterol stones.
items.explanation: The UGT1A1 gene variant is associated with an increased presence of cholesterol stones.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.question: Which demographic group has the highest prevalence of cholesterol gallstones?
options: North American Indians, Chilean Indians, and Chilean Hispanics
items.explanation: Cholesterol stones have the highest prevalence in North American Indians, Chilean Indians, and Chilean Hispanics.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.question: Which factor contributes to increased biliary cholesterol secretion during pregnancy?
options: Combined effect of progesterine and estrogens
items.explanation: During pregnancy, the combined influence of progesterone (causing impaired gallbladder emptying) and estrogens (increasing biliary cholesterol secretion) contributes to increased risk.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.question: What is a consequence of rapid weight loss on bile composition?
options: Increased enterohepatic circulation of bile acids
Mobilization of tissue cholesterol leading to increased biliary cholesterol secretion
Stabilized size of the bile acid pool
items.explanation: Rapid weight loss leads to mobilization of tissue cholesterol, increasing biliary cholesterol secretion while decreasing enterohepatic circulation of bile acids.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.question: Which demographic factor is associated with an increased prevalence of pigment stones due to parasitic biliary infections?
items.explanation: Pigment stone formation is frequent in Asia and often associated with parasitic infections.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.question: What condition can lead to the formation of pigment stones due to ineffective erythropoiesis?
items.explanation: Ineffective erythropoiesis, as seen in pernicious anemia, can lead to pigment stone formation.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is most accurate for identifying cholelithiasis and has replaced oral cholecystography?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography of the gallbladder is very accurate in identifying cholelithiasis and has replaced oral cholecystography.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.question: What symptom is most characteristic of gallstone disease?
items.explanation: The most specific and characteristic symptom of gallstone disease is biliary colic.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.question: What is the yearly incidence of complications in patients with asymptomatic gallstones?
items.explanation: The passage states that the yearly incidence of complications is about 0.1–0.3%.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: Which factor does NOT typically warrant a recommendation for cholecystectomy in asymptomatic gallstone patients?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that few authorities recommend routine cholecystectomy for silent stones in young patients.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the approximate rate of bile duct injuries during laparoscopic cholecystectomy?
items.explanation: The passage states that the rate of bile duct injuries is low, at 0.2–0.6%, and comparable with open cholecystectomy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: Which type of gallstones is not responsive to UDCA therapy?
items.explanation: Pigment stones are not responsive to UDCA therapy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.question: What is the recommended dose of UDCA for gallstone dissolution?
items.explanation: The recommended dose of UDCA is 10–15 mg/kg per day.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.question: What size of radiolucent gallstones are considered for dissolution therapy to achieve good results within a reasonable time period?
items.explanation: For good results, the therapy should be limited to radiolucent stones <5 mm in diameter.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.question: Which organisms are most frequently isolated by culture of gallbladder bile in patients with acute cholecystitis?
options: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella spp., Streptococcus spp., and Clostridium spp.
Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterococcus faecalis
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Haemophilus influenzae
Candida albicans, Aspergillus fumigatus, Cryptococcus neoformans
items.explanation: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella spp., Streptococcus spp., and Clostridium spp. are the most frequently isolated organisms in acute cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic sign of acute cholecystitis during subcostal palpation?
items.explanation: Murphy's sign is characterized by increased pain and inspiratory arrest during subcostal palpation of the RUQ.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of patients with acute cholecystitis experience remission of symptoms within 2–7 days following hospitalization?
items.explanation: Approximately 75% of patients treated medically have remission of acute symptoms within 2–7 days.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is useful for detecting signs of gallbladder inflammation including thickening of the wall and pericholecystic fluid?
items.explanation: Ultrasound is useful for detecting signs of gallbladder inflammation such as thickening of the wall and pericholecystic fluid.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging finding is most characteristic of acalculous cholecystitis?
options: A large, tense, static gallbladder without stones
items.explanation: Ultrasound or CT showing a large, tense, static gallbladder without stones is diagnostic for acalculous cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary factor in successfully managing acute acalculous cholecystitis?
items.explanation: Successful management depends primarily on early diagnosis and surgical intervention.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which bacteria are most frequently cultured in emphysematous cholecystitis?
options: Clostridium welchii or C. perfringens and E. coli
items.explanation: Anaerobes like Clostridium welchii or C. perfringens, and aerobes such as E. coli are commonly cultured.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.question: What condition may result from prolonged cystic duct obstruction by a large solitary calculus?
options: Mucocele or hydrops of the gallbladder
items.explanation: Prolonged cystic duct obstruction by a large solitary calculus can lead to mucocele or hydrops.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which organ is most commonly involved in fistula formation due to acute cholecystitis?
items.explanation: Fistulas into the duodenum are most common in cases of acute cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.question: What is the typical site of obstruction in gallstone ileus?
items.explanation: The site of obstruction by the impacted gallstone is usually at the ileocecal valve.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.question: What condition may be diagnosed by finding gas in the biliary tree on plain abdominal films?
items.explanation: Asymptomatic cholecystoenteric fistulas may be diagnosed by finding gas in the biliary tree on plain abdominal films.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of initial medical management for acute cholecystitis?
items.explanation: Initial management includes eliminating oral intake, nasogastric suction if needed, and intravenous antibiotics; immediate oral antibiotics are not used.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.question: Intravenous antibiotic therapy is indicated in patients with severe acute cholecystitis primarily because:
options: Bacterial superinfection of bile has occurred
It is a standard protocol for all cases
items.explanation: Antibiotic therapy helps reduce postoperative complications like wound infection, abscess formation, and sepsis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic combination is NOT mentioned as effective for treating severe acute cholecystitis?
items.explanation: The passage does not mention vancomycin plus ciprofloxacin as an effective antibiotic combination.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the recommended timing for early elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy in patients with uncomplicated acute cholecystitis?
items.explanation: Early elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is ideally performed within 48–72 hours after the diagnosis of uncomplicated acute cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which patients are best reserved for delayed surgical intervention in cases of acute cholecystitis?
options: Patients with a high risk due to overall medical condition
All patients over the age of 60
items.explanation: Delayed surgical intervention is best reserved for patients whose overall medical condition imposes an unacceptable risk for early surgery.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What are the mortality rates associated with emergency cholecystectomy and early elective cholecystectomy in most centers?
options: Both procedures have a mortality rate of 1-3%
items.explanation: The mortality figures for emergency cholecystectomy range from 1 to 3%, whereas early elective cholecystectomy has a mortality risk of ~0.5% in patients under age 60.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which postcholecystectomy syndrome is attributed to symptoms resembling biliary pain or cholecystitis due to a long cystic duct remnant?
items.explanation: Cystic duct stump syndrome is attributed to symptoms resembling biliary pain or cholecystitis due to a long cystic duct remnant.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following criteria are used to characterize biliary SOD disorders?
options: (1) Biliary pain, (2) Absence of bile duct stones or other abnormalities, and (3) Elevated liver enzymes or a dilated CBD, but not both
(1) Biliary pain, (2) Presence of bile duct stones, and (3) Elevated liver enzymes
(1) Abdominal pain, (2) Dilated CBD, and (3) Normal liver enzymes
(1) Absence of biliary pain, (2) Normal bile ducts, and (3) Decreased liver enzymes
items.explanation: Biliary SOD disorders are characterized by the presence of biliary pain, absence of bile duct stones or other abnormalities, and either elevated liver enzymes or a dilated CBD, but not both.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended course of action for patients with SOD dyskinesia who do not respond to medical therapy?
options: Perform endoscopic biliary sphincterotomy (EBS) or surgical sphincterotomy
items.explanation: Endoscopic biliary sphincterotomy (EBS) or surgical sphincterotomy may be indicated in patients who fail to respond to a trial of medical therapy, especially if SOD pressures are elevated.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients typically experience long-term pain relief after undergoing EBS for SOD dyskinesia?
items.explanation: Approximately 45% of patients have long-term pain relief after undergoing endoscopic biliary sphincterotomy (EBS).
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: Which feature is characteristic of adenomyomatosis?
options: Abnormal deposition of lipid within macrophages in the lamina propria
Benign proliferation of gallbladder surface epithelium with glandlike formations
Solitary or multiple cholesterol polyps studding the gallbladder wall
Diffuse form with brick red mucosa and yellow flecks
items.explanation: Adenomyomatosis is characterized by benign proliferation of gallbladder surface epithelium.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.question: What is the recommended management for asymptomatic patients with gallbladder polyps <6 mm?
items.explanation: Asymptomatic patients with polyps <6 mm show no significant changes over time, so no intervention is needed unless symptoms develop.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.question: Under what condition is cholecystectomy recommended for patients with cholesterolosis?
options: Presence of cholesterol stones in the gallbladder
For all cases regardless of symptoms
If polyps are between 10 and 14 mm
items.explanation: Cholecystectomy is indicated for cholesterolosis when biliary symptoms are present.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.statement: The total daily basal secretion of hepatic bile is approximately 500–600 mL.
items.explanation: The passage states that the total daily basal secretion of hepatic bile is ~500–600 mL.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.statement: Cholangiocellular secretion involves active transport of bile acids from hepatocytes into the bile canaliculi.
items.explanation: Cholangiocellular secretion is a secretin-mediated, cyclic AMP-dependent mechanism for bicarbonate-rich fluid secretion, not bile acid transport.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.statement: Bile acids are absorbed only by passive diffusion along the entire gut.
items.explanation: While unconjugated bile acids are absorbed by passive diffusion, conjugated bile acids in the distal ileum use active transport.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.statement: The maximum rate of hepatic synthesis of bile acids is ~5 g/d.
items.explanation: The maximum rate of synthesis of bile acids by the liver is indeed ~5 g/d.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.statement: A 'floating' gallbladder is a congenital anomaly that predisposes to acute torsion, volvulus, or herniation.
items.explanation: 'Floating' gallbladder refers to anomalies of position or suspension that can lead to complications like torsion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.statement: The sphincter of Oddi prevents the flow of bile into the duodenum.
items.explanation: The sphincter of Oddi controls bile flow into the duodenum by offering resistance, not preventing it.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.statement: Cholesterol gallstones usually contain less than 20% cholesterol.
items.explanation: Cholesterol gallstones usually contain >50% cholesterol monohydrate.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.statement: Genetic factors account for a significant portion of the phenotypic variation in gallstone disease among monozygotic twins.
items.explanation: Genetic factors accounted for 25% of the phenotypic variation among monozygotic twins.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.statement: Biliary sludge can develop due to gallbladder hypomotility.
items.explanation: The passage states that biliary sludge can develop with disorders causing gallbladder hypomotility.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.statement: Pregnancy-related changes in bile composition and gallbladder function are permanent.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the abnormalities related to pregnancy reverse rapidly after delivery.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.statement: Estrogens decrease the uptake of dietary cholesterol by hepatic lipoprotein receptors.
items.explanation: Estrogens stimulate hepatic lipoprotein receptors, increasing the uptake of dietary cholesterol.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.statement: Clofibrate therapy leads to decreased biliary secretion of cholesterol.
items.explanation: Clofibrate therapy actually increases the biliary secretion of cholesterol.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.statement: OCG is still a commonly used procedure for diagnosing gallstones.
items.explanation: OCG has been replaced by ultrasound and is now regarded as obsolete.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.statement: Biliary colic pain typically subsides within a few hours.
items.explanation: Biliary colic begins quite suddenly and may persist with severe intensity for 30 min to 5 h, subsiding gradually or rapidly.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.statement: Prophylactic cholecystectomy is recommended for all asymptomatic gallstone patients.
items.explanation: The passage suggests that prophylactic cholecystectomy is not warranted in most cases of asymptomatic gallstones.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.statement: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy has a higher rate of bile duct injuries compared to open cholecystectomy.
items.explanation: The passage states that the rate of bile duct injuries is low and comparable with open cholecystectomy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 2) | SURGICAL THERAPY

items.statement: Cholesterol gallstone disease patients who develop recurrent choledocholithiasis after cholecystectomy should be on long-term treatment with UDCA.
items.explanation: Patients with cholesterol gallstone disease and recurrent choledocholithiasis post-cholecystectomy are recommended for long-term UDCA therapy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.statement: Stones larger than 10 mm in diameter frequently dissolve with UDCA treatment.
items.explanation: Stones >10 mm in size rarely dissolve with UDCA therapy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.statement: Jaundice is a common early symptom in acute cholecystitis.
items.explanation: Jaundice is unusual early in the course of acute cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Acalculous cholecystitis can be associated with prolonged fasting and serious trauma.
items.explanation: Prolonged fasting and serious trauma are risk factors for acalculous cholecystitis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Chronic cholecystitis is almost always associated with the presence of gallstones.
items.explanation: Chronic inflammation of the gallbladder wall is almost always linked to gallstones.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.statement: Empyema of the gallbladder carries a low risk of gram-negative sepsis.
items.explanation: Empyema carries a high risk of gram-negative sepsis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.statement: Laparotomy with stone extraction is the procedure of choice to relieve gallstone ileus.
items.explanation: Laparotomy with stone extraction remains the procedure of choice for relieving obstruction in gallstone ileus.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.statement: Cholecystectomy is always required for patients with porcelain gallbladder.
items.explanation: The need for cholecystectomy in porcelain gallbladder is not absolute; close surveillance is also acceptable.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 3)

items.statement: Antibiotic therapy is only indicated after bacterial superinfection of bile has been confirmed in acute cholecystitis.
items.explanation: Antibiotics are given even if bacterial superinfection has not occurred, to prevent complications.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.statement: Effective antibiotics for severe acute cholecystitis include piperacillin plus tazobactam and ceftriaxone plus metronidazole.
items.explanation: Both combinations are mentioned as effective in the passage.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY

items.statement: The complication rate increases in patients undergoing early cholecystectomy compared to delayed cholecystectomy.
items.explanation: The complication rate is not increased in patients undergoing early as opposed to delayed cholecystectomy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Percutaneous drainage can be used for seriously ill or debilitated patients with cholecystitis.
items.explanation: Serious or debilitated patients may be managed with percutaneous drainage, among other methods.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Firm data linking dyspepsia symptoms to bile gastritis after cholecystectomy are abundant.
items.explanation: Firm data linking these symptoms to bile gastritis after surgical removal of the gallbladder are lacking.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Cholecystectomy can lead to a shift in bile acid composition towards more diarrheagenic secondary bile acids.
items.explanation: Cholecystectomy shortens gut transit time, causing an increase in colonic bile acid output and a shift toward more diarrheagenic secondary bile acids.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Adenomyomatosis involves the formation of cholesterol polyps.
items.explanation: Adenomyomatosis is characterized by proliferation of gallbladder surface epithelium, not cholesterol polyps.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.statement: Cholecystectomy is recommended for asymptomatic patients with polyps >10 mm.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic patients over 50 years with polyps >10 mm are advised to undergo cholecystectomy.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.assertion: The electrolyte composition of gallbladder bile differs from that of hepatic bile.
items.reason: Most inorganic anions, chloride, and bicarbonate are removed by reabsorption across the gallbladder epithelium.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the electrolyte composition of gallbladder bile differs from hepatic bile.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION
items.assertion: Mutations in MDR3 (ABCB4) result in Dubin-Johnson syndrome.
items.reason: Dubin-Johnson syndrome is caused by defective anionic conjugate export pump (MRP2, ABCC2).
items.explanation: The assertion is false because mutations in MDR3 (ABCB4) result in PFIC3, not Dubin-Johnson syndrome. The reason correctly identifies the cause of Dubin-Johnson syndrome.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts | BILE SECRETION AND COMPOSITION

items.assertion: Bile acids stimulate the release of fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19).
items.reason: FGF19 suppresses hepatic synthesis of bile acids from cholesterol.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and FGF19 indeed regulates hepatic bile acid synthesis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.assertion: Normal ratios of bile acids and lecithin promote the precipitation of cholesterol crystals.
items.reason: Cholesterol solubility in bile depends on total lipid concentration and relative molar percentages of bile acids and lecithin.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; normal ratios favor micelle formation, not precipitation. The reason is true.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE BILE ACIDS | ENTEROHEPATIC CIRCULATION

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES
items.assertion: Cholecystokinin (CCK) promotes the filling of the gallbladder.
items.reason: The tonic contraction of the sphincter of Oddi prevents reflux and promotes gallbladder filling.
items.explanation: While CCK causes gallbladder contraction, it is the sphincter of Oddi's tonic contraction that promotes gallbladder filling.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLBLADDER AND SPHINCTERIC FUNCTIONS | CONGENITAL ANOMALIES

items.assertion: Increased biliary secretion of cholesterol is a key mechanism in lithogenic bile formation.
items.reason: This may occur due to obesity, high-caloric diets, or genetic factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.assertion: Most individuals with supersaturated bile develop gallstones.
items.reason: Supersaturation of bile with cholesterol is a sufficient condition for stone formation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; most individuals with supersaturated bile do not develop stones.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 1)

items.assertion: Cholesterol gallstone disease occurs due to bile supersaturation with cholesterol.
items.reason: Bile supersaturation leads to nucleation of cholesterol monohydrate crystals, which is a key step in stone formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; bile supersaturation facilitates crystal nucleation, contributing to gallstone disease.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.assertion: Ultrasonographic studies show no difference in gallbladder volume between patients with and without gallstones.
items.reason: Patients with gallstones exhibit an increased residual gallbladder volume after a meal.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ultrasonographic studies do show differences, as patients with gallstones have increased gallbladder volumes.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 2)

items.assertion: Obesity is associated with increased biliary secretion of cholesterol.
items.reason: Normal bile acid pool and secretion but increased biliary secretion of cholesterol in obese individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association between obesity and increased biliary cholesterol secretion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · GALLSTONES (part 3) | Cholesterol Stones

items.assertion: Chronic hemolysis can lead to pigment stone formation.
items.reason: Conditions like sickle cell disease cause chronic hemolysis, contributing to pigment stone formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.assertion: Ultrasonography can distinguish between biliary sludge and gallstones.
items.reason: Biliary sludge produces acoustic shadowing, unlike gallstones.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; biliary sludge fails to produce acoustic shadowing.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · Pigment Stones (part 1)

items.assertion: The role of gallstone dissolution has been largely reduced due to the advantages and success of laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
items.reason: UDCA therapy is effective for all types of gallstones.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as UDCA is not effective for pigment stones.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · MEDICAL THERAPY—GALLSTONE DISSOLUTION

items.assertion: Acute cholecystitis is best treated by early surgery whenever possible.
items.reason: The natural history of the disease shows a high recurrence rate without surgical intervention.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early surgery is recommended.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Mirizzi's syndrome involves a gallstone causing compression of the common bile duct.
items.reason: Ultrasound shows gallstones lying inside the hepatic duct in Mirizzi's syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; ultrasound shows gallstones lying outside the hepatic duct.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Gangrene of the gallbladder often leads to perforation.
items.reason: Ischemia and necrosis predispose the gallbladder wall to rupture.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Hydrops of the gallbladder frequently presents with severe RUQ pain.
items.reason: The condition involves progressive distention by mucus or transudate.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hydrops often remains asymptomatic, though chronic RUQ pain may occur.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · ACUTE AND CHRONIC CHOLECYSTITIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Early cholecystectomy is the treatment of choice for most patients with acute cholecystitis.
items.reason: Early cholecystectomy does not increase complication rates compared to delayed surgery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early cholecystectomy is preferred.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Cystic duct stump syndrome is a common cause of persistent postcholecystectomy symptoms.
items.reason: Symptoms are often attributable to other causes rather than the cystic duct stump itself.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because cystic duct stump syndrome is not commonly responsible for persistent symptoms; the reason is true.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · SURGICAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Cholesterolosis is characterized by the deposition of lipid within macrophages.
items.reason: This leads to a brick red gallbladder mucosa with yellow flecks in its diffuse form.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the characteristic appearance of cholesterolosis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Gallbladder and Bile Ducts · THE HYPERPLASTIC CHOLECYSTOSES
